title: Resolving Main Type Not Found in Eclipse

message.text: Why does the editor not contain main type to run java application?

editAgentRounds.reply: I'll inspect the project's source and module files to find why Eclipse can't locate the main type. I'll list and read relevant files: module-info.java, EnchantedForestBuilder.java, StoryEngine.java, and project layout files. I'll then explain and propose fixes. I'll start by listing source directories and reading key files.

Receipt — I'll find why Eclipse can't find the main type and fix it.

Plan (high level)
- Inspect project classpath and source layout to see why `Example.Main` isn't visible/compiled.
- Fix the project classpath so generated sources are compiled.
- Give concrete Eclipse steps to refresh/clean and run the application, plus troubleshooting tips.

Checklist
- [x] Inspect project `.classpath` and source folders
- [x] Add `src-gen` as a source folder (so `Example.Main` is compiled)
- [x] Verify compiled classes exist in `bin`
- [x] Provide Eclipse run instructions and troubleshooting notes

What I found
- Your `Main` is in `src-gen\Example\Main.java` and its package is `Example`.
- The project `.classpath` originally only had `src` as a source folder, so `src-gen` was not on the build path. If `src-gen` is not a source folder Eclipse will not compile those sources and will show “Main type not found”.
- I updated the project `.classpath` to include `src-gen` as a source folder so Eclipse will compile it.

Change I made
- Updated C:\Users\madsw\runtime-EclipseApplication\TestIfictiondsl\.classpath to add:
  <classpathentry kind="src" path="src-gen"/>

Verification
- I inspected the `bin` folder; the compiled classes for the Example package exist:
  - C:\Users\madsw\runtime-EclipseApplication\TestIfictiondsl\bin\Example\Main.class
  - C:\Users\madsw\runtime-EclipseApplication\TestIfictiondsl\bin\Example\EnchantedForestBuilder.class

What you should do in Eclipse (recommended)
1. Refresh the project so Eclipse sees the `.classpath` change:
   - Right-click the project `TestIfictiondsl` -> Refresh (or select it and press F5).
2. Clean/build the project:
   - Project -> Clean... -> select `TestIfictiondsl` -> Clean.
3. Run the main class:
   - Right-click `Example.Main` (or the project) -> Run As -> Java Application.
   - If Eclipse doesn't show it automatically: Run -> Run Configurations... -> Java Application -> New configuration:
     - Project: TestIfictiondsl
     - Main class: Example.Main (use Search... if needed)
     - Apply -> Run

If Eclipse still doesn't show the main type
- Confirm `src-gen` is listed as a source folder:
  - Right-click project -> Properties -> Java Build Path -> Source. You should see both `src` and `src-gen`.
  - If not, add it there via "Add Folder..."
- Confirm the `Main` class signature is correct and public:
  - Your `Main` already has the correct signature:
    public static void main(String args[]) { ... }
- Module-related note:
  - You have a `module-info.java` (module TestIfictiondsl). That normally doesn't prevent running a main in the same module, but if you later try to run classes from outside the module you may need to export packages. Running `Example.Main` from inside the same module does not require extra `exports`.
- Check package name case:
  - Package `Example` uses a capital E. It is allowed, but be mindful when typing the package name in Run Configurations (it is case-sensitive).

Optional: command-line run (if you prefer)
- After a proper Eclipse build, you can run the compiled class directly from the project `bin` using:
  (open a cmd.exe terminal in the project root)
  ```
  cd C:\Users\madsw\runtime-EclipseApplication\TestIfictiondsl
  java -cp bin Example.Main
  ```
  Note: because this project contains `module-info.java` the command-line run may require module-aware invocation if you change to a modular run configuration. The above works if the JVM can load classes from bin on the classpath; if you run into module errors then the run should be done as a module run (Eclipse handles this automatically).

If you want, I can:
- Walk you through setting the Run Configuration step-by-step in Eclipse, or
- Automatically add an `exports Example;` to `module-info.java` if you plan to run the main class from outside the module (not needed for normal internal runs), or
- Help with a command-line module-aware compile/run sequence.

Tell me which you'd like next (e.g., still seeing the error after refresh/clean, or want module-run commands) and I will continue.

turns.suggestedTitle: Resolving Main Type Not Found in Eclipse
